structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a major hospital system spanning 5 years, containing 50,000+ patient admissions. Data includes admission/discharge timestamps, demographic information, treatment history, clinical notes (unstructured text), laboratory results, and medications. Data is sourced from the production EHR system and represents the deployment target population.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 days of discharge. Readmission is defined as any acute care admission occurring ≥24 hours and ≤30 days after the index discharge date, identified via EHR admission records. Readmissions for planned procedures (identified via admission type codes) are excluded. Label is determined prospectively from historical records, with no look-ahead bias.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal stratified split. Index admissions are ordered chronologically by discharge date. The first 60% (by discharge date, ~30,000 admissions) form the training set; the next 20% (~10,000 admissions, temporally after training) form the validation set; the final 20% (~10,000 admissions) form the test set. Within each temporal segment, stratification by readmission class (Yes/No) is applied to preserve class balance. Temporal splitting prevents information leakage: no patient's future clinical trajectory or outcomes influence model training. Stratification ensures each set has representative label distributions despite potential class imbalance.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, one-hot encoding for categorical variables, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set. Fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents test-set statistics from influencing model development. For clinical notes, the vocabulary and TF-IDF vectorizer are fit on training-set notes only, then applied to validation and test notes. Any missing values in features are imputed using training-set statistics (e.g., median age, most common department); no information from validation or test sets informs imputation.
structured_choices.features: Two feature groups are constructed: (1) Structured features: age, sex, race/ethnicity, admission type, primary diagnosis (ICD code), comorbidity count, prior readmissions in past 12 months, length of stay, number of medications at discharge, laboratory values (e.g., hemoglobin, creatinine, last recorded values), and temporal features (day of week, season of discharge). (2) Unstructured features: clinical notes (admission notes, progress notes, discharge summaries) are preprocessed via lowercasing, removal of protected health information patterns, tokenization, and conversion to TF-IDF vectors (top 500 features by document frequency). No feature is derived from the readmission outcome itself. All features are measured or recorded during the index admission or prior to discharge—no post-discharge information is included to avoid leakage.
structured_choices.model: Ensemble model combining: (A) a gradient boosted tree model (XGBoost) trained on structured features only, and (B) a logistic regression model trained on TF-IDF features from clinical notes. The ensemble prediction is the weighted average of the two model probabilities (weights to be tuned on validation set, initialized at 0.5 each). This ensemble design tests whether the addition of clinical notes (via model B) and the combination mechanism improve predictions over structured data alone.
structured_choices.baseline: Single logistic regression model trained on structured features only (no clinical notes). The baseline receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort as the ensemble: learning rate, regularization strength (L2 penalty), and convergence tolerance are tuned via grid search on the validation set using the primary metric. Both the baseline and ensemble use the same structured feature set and preprocessing pipeline. The baseline represents the current standard approach (historical treatment data only) and is the appropriate comparison to isolate the contribution of clinical notes and ensemble methodology.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: Readmission prediction is a decision-support tool where clinicians will apply a threshold to flag high-risk patients for intervention. AUROC measures discriminative ability across all thresholds, is robust to class imbalance (a common issue in readmission datasets), and aligns with stakeholder objectives: better discrimination allows clinicians to identify and prevent more readmissions. AUROC is also threshold-independent, avoiding arbitrary choices about sensitivity/specificity trade-offs at the model development stage.
secondary_metrics: Precision-Recall AUC: captures performance in the positive class (readmitted patients), important for resource allocation in intervention programs.
Calibration (Expected Calibration Error, ECE): ensures predicted probabilities reflect true risk, critical for clinical decision-making.
Sensitivity at specificity ≥0.85: measures true positive rate when false positive rate is controlled, representing a clinically feasible operating point.
Positive Predictive Value (PPV) at threshold optimized for F1 score on validation set: balances precision and recall for practical deployment.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: The validation set (20% of data, temporally after training) is used exclusively for model selection. Hyperparameters for both the baseline and ensemble are tuned via grid search, evaluating each configuration on the validation set using the primary metric (AUROC). Ensemble weights are optimized on the validation set. The test set is held out and touched only once: after all model and hyperparameter decisions are finalized, the test set is used exactly once to report final performance metrics. No iterative refinement or threshold adjustment is performed on the test set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is used exactly once, at the end of the experiment, to generate the final performance report. No exploratory analysis, metric calculation, or model retuning occurs on the test set. Results on the test set are the definitive evaluation of the hypothesis. If additional analyses (e.g., subgroup performance) are desired, they must be pre-specified before test-set evaluation and reported transparently.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Demographic stratification: performance is evaluated separately for subgroups defined by age (≤65, >65), sex (male, female), and race/ethnicity to detect whether the ensemble's advantage varies by patient demographics. If the ensemble consistently outperforms the baseline across all demographic subgroups, confounding by these factors is less likely. Health system policy controls: if data includes multiple hospital campuses or departments, stratified analysis is performed to verify that ensemble improvements are not driven by variation in policies or data quality across sites. Temporal stability: performance is evaluated in rolling windows (e.g., months 16–17 vs. months 17–18 of the test period) to detect whether the ensemble advantage persists over time or erodes due to concept drift. Baseline comparison at equal tuning effort: the baseline receives the same hyperparameter optimization budget (grid search scope, validation-set evaluation) as the ensemble, ensuring the comparison is fair and confounding by differential tuning effort is ruled out.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Ensemble Methods for Readmission Risk Prediction

### Objective
To test whether an ensemble combining gradient-boosted models on historical treatment data with logistic regression on clinical notes outperforms a baseline logistic regression model trained on historical data alone, in predicting 30-day patient readmission risk.

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy
The experiment uses electronic health records from a major hospital system spanning 5 years, encompassing 50,000+ patient admissions. This data represents the deployment target and contains all required information: structured EHR fields (demographics, diagnoses, medications, lab values, admission timing), unstructured clinical notes (admission, progress, and discharge notes), and outcome data (readmission events).

The target variable is defined prospectively: readmission within 30 days of discharge. Any acute care admission occurring ≥24 hours and ≤30 days after discharge is marked as readmission; planned readmissions (elective procedures identified via admission codes) are excluded. This definition aligns with hospital quality metrics and CMS reporting standards, ensuring the model targets clinically and operationally relevant outcomes.

### 2. Split Strategy and Leakage Prevention
Data is split temporally by discharge date: training (first 60%, ~30,000 admissions), validation (next 20%, ~10,000 admissions), and test (final 20%, ~10,000 admissions). Within each temporal segment, stratification by readmission class preserves label distribution.

Temporal splitting is essential because patient outcomes and clinical trajectories develop over time. A random split would allow a model trained on later data to see patterns from earlier clinical notes or treatment changes that occurred after the index admission, creating information leakage. Temporal ordering ensures that the training set contains only information available at or before discharge, and the validation and test sets represent true future predictions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Structured features include: age, sex, race/ethnicity, admission type, primary diagnosis (ICD code), comorbidity burden, prior readmission history, length of stay, discharge medication count, and laboratory values recorded at admission or during the stay. Temporal features (day of week, season) capture time-dependent patterns.

Clinical notes are preprocessed via lowercasing, removal of protected health information patterns, tokenization, and TF-IDF vectorization (top 500 features). All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on the training set, then applied to validation and test sets. This ensures test-set statistics do not influence model development. No feature is derived from the readmission outcome or post-discharge information.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The proposed ensemble combines two models:
- **Model A (Structured):** XGBoost trained on structured features only.
- **Model B (Clinical Notes):** Logistic regression trained on TF-IDF vectors from clinical notes.
- **Ensemble:** Weighted average of Model A and Model B predictions (weights tuned on validation set).

The baseline is a logistic regression model trained on structured features only, representing current practice (historical data without clinical notes). Both the ensemble and baseline receive identical hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search on the validation set, using the primary metric (AUROC). This ensures the comparison is fair and isolates the contribution of clinical notes and ensemble methodology.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation
The validation set is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning. Grid search over hyperparameter configurations (learning rate, regularization, ensemble weights) is performed, and the configuration with the highest AUROC on the validation set is selected. The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once, after all model decisions are finalized, to produce the definitive performance report.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric: AUROC.** Readmission prediction is a decision-support task where clinicians apply thresholds to identify high-risk patients for intervention. AUROC measures discriminative ability across all thresholds, is robust to class imbalance, and directly aligns with stakeholder objectives: better discrimination enables more effective identification and prevention of readmissions.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision-Recall AUC (performance in the positive class), calibration error (probability reliability for clinical decisions), sensitivity at high specificity (operating-point performance), and PPV at F1-optimized thresholds (practical deployment effectiveness).

### 7. Controls for Confounds
Demographic stratification: Performance is evaluated separately by age, sex, and race/ethnicity to verify that ensemble improvements are consistent across demographic groups and not driven by demographic confounding. Health system variation: Rolling analysis across hospital sites and time windows checks for robustness. Baseline fairness: Equal tuning effort for baseline and ensemble prevents confounding by differential model optimization. These controls establish whether the ensemble advantage is genuine and generalizable or artifacts of specific populations or time periods.
